Mojave Nature by Kurt Buzard


Linanthus demissus

Desertsnow

A tiny, ground-hugging annual that blankets desert flats with delicate white flowers, appearing like a dusting of snow. Linanthus demissus is a classic belly flower, so named because one often has to get down on their belly to appreciate its intricate beauty. The flowers are typically white with five lobes and are often marked with purple lines or a yellowish center that guides pollinators to the nectar.

Learn More Details About This Plant

Plant Data

Names and Taxonomy

  • Common Names: desertsnow, humble desert-star
  • Synonyms: gilia demissa
  • Family: polemoniaceae
  • Genus Information: CA 33, NV 13, AZ 10, UT 9, NM 3

Habit and Size

  • Duration: annual herb
  • Habit: prostrate to ascending
  • Shape: tufted / mat-forming
  • Size: 1–5 cm tall
  • Stem: very short, branched, often reddish

Leaves

  • Color: green to grayish-green
  • Shape: linear to lanceolate
  • Edge: entire
  • Arrangement: opposite (lower) to alternate (upper)
  • Leaf Retention: annual

Flowers and Fruit

  • Sex: hermaphroditic
  • Flower Color: white, often with yellowish or purple throat markings
  • Flower Size: 5–8 mm long
  • Number of Petals: 5
  • Bloom: March–May
  • Fruit: small capsule

Habitat and Growing Conditions

  • Water Use: low
  • Light: full sun
  • Soil: sandy or gravelly desert soils
  • Habitat: creosote bush scrub, open desert flats

Distribution

  • Distribution: CA, NV, AZ, UT
  • Seen: Temple Bar
  • Elevation: 1000–5000 feet (300–1525 meters)

Gallery

Linanthus demissus

Observed as a white carpet across sandy washes in the low desert.
